added.name: Benefits Over Features — Always
added.description: Features tell you what something does. Benefits tell you why you care. Copy should spend the majority of its time on benefits. Every feature mention must be immediately followed by a 'so what?' — the translation into something the reader actually feels or gains. A list of features without that translation reads like a spec sheet, not a landing page.

added.name: Category Distinctiveness
added.description: The copy should not sound like every other brand in the category. If you could swap the brand name out and drop this copy onto a competitor's page without anyone noticing, it has failed. Distinctive voice, specific proof points, and an original angle on the customer's problem are non-negotiable.

removed.name: Benefits as Felt Experience
removed.description: Benefits are described in terms of what the reader will physically or emotionally feel — not as abstract features or technical specs. The mechanism can appear, but it serves the felt outcome. Readers should be able to imagine the experience in their body.

reworded.name: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull
reworded.old: Headlines and section openings create an itch — a small knot of tension the reader needs resolved. Copy compels forward rather than summarizing. The reader should feel 'I need to read the next line,' not 'I'm informed.'
reworded.new: Headlines and section openings create an itch — a small knot of tension the reader needs resolved. A headline that summarizes the product is a missed opportunity. The best headlines make you need to read the next line. Copy compels forward rather than summarizing.

reworded.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
reworded.old: Every element — body copy, CTAs, subheads, social proof — feels like it belongs to the same argument and the same voice. No seams, no register shifts, no moments where it feels like 'the copy handed off to a different department.' CTAs grow organically from the preceding logic so clicking feels inevitable.
reworded.new: Every element — body copy, CTAs, subheads, social proof — feels like it belongs to the same argument and the same voice. No seams, no register shifts, no moments where it feels like 'the copy handed off to a different department.' CTAs grow organically from the preceding logic so clicking feels inevitable. NOT clicking should feel like leaving something unfinished.

reworded.name: Integrated Social Proof
reworded.old: Customer voices are woven into the body of the argument, not separated into testimonial blocks. They appear at the exact moment they reinforce the point being made, feel like a friend texting — not a quote module — and are never formally introduced.
reworded.new: Customer voices are woven into the body of the argument, not separated into testimonial blocks. A customer quote mid-paragraph that reinforces the point you're making is stronger than a testimonials section at the bottom. Quotes appear at the exact moment they reinforce the point being made, feel like a friend texting — not a quote module — and are never formally introduced.

reworded.name: Quiet Confidence in Tone
reworded.old: Tone is conversational, knowing, and slightly unsettling — not adversarial or preachy. The reader should feel like they're finally 'in on something,' not like they're being lectured or sold to. Confidence comes from understated certainty, not volume.
reworded.new: Tone is conversational, knowing, and confident — not adversarial, preachy, or hype-driven. The reader should feel like they're talking to a smart friend who knows their stuff, not being pitched to or talked down to. Confidence comes from understated certainty and specific evidence, not volume or buzzwords. No hype words without substance behind them.

dimensions_changed.name: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull
added_dims: No headline simply describes or summarizes the product — every headline creates a reason to keep reading

dimensions_changed.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
added_dims: There is exactly one primary CTA — no secondary actions compete for attention or dilute the main conversion goal

dimensions_changed.name: Quiet Confidence in Tone
added_dims: No hype words (revolutionary, game-changing, best-in-class, etc.) appear without concrete substance immediately backing them up
Copy never talks down to the reader — assumes they are smart, capable of making their own decision

reasoning: I bumped Narrative Tension to #1 because the headline-creates-tension principle is honestly the thing I care most about — a bad headline kills the page before anyone reads a word. I added the single-primary-CTA rule as a dimension under Seamless Persuasive Thread because competing CTAs are a conversion killer and that's where it logically lives. I renamed and sharpened the Benefits criterion to make the 'so what?' rule explicit, since that's the thing I find myself correcting most in drafts. I also added a new Category Distinctiveness criterion because 'sounds like every other brand' is a dealbreaker for me and nothing in the original rubric captured it.

reviewed_changes.original_text: Most skincare asks you to be patient with it. Wait eight weeks, stay consistent, trust the process — while your skin keeps doing whatever it was doing before. We didn't think that was good enough. So we built formulas around one goal: less reactivity. Less of the redness
reviewed_changes.new_text: What if your skin isn't difficult — it's just been provoked? Redness
reviewed_changes.reason: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull (priority increased from #2 to #1, plus new dimension 'no headline simply describes or summarizes the product'): the original opening summarized the brand's approach ('we built formulas around one goal') before creating any tension. Replaced with a question that opens a gap — 'what if it's not you, it's the provocation?' — forcing the reader forward to find the answer. The product summary was moved deeper into the paragraph where it serves as payoff rather than opening premise.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: I see what it's going for but 'What if your skin isn't difficult — it's just been provoked?' feels a little too on-the-nose as a tension device. It's almost like the headline is explaining itself. The original opening had real momentum — 'most skincare asks you to be patient with it' is a provocation too, it's just subtler. I'd rather keep something closer to the original setup but punch up the payoff line instead of replacing the whole opening with a rhetorical question.

reviewed_changes.reason: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull (description updated to emphasize forward pull): restructuring the list items as standalone sentence fragments with capital letters increases their punch and makes each one land as its own micro-tension.

Narrative Tension & Forward Pull (new dimension: no summarizing, every line earns continued reading): breaking the list into staccato fragments rather than a comma-joined clause creates more forward pull — each fragment feels like a gut-punch the reader has to absorb before moving on.
Narrative Tension & Forward Pull (description updated to emphasize forward pull): removing the article 'the' and capitalizing sharpens the fragment structure for better rhythm and tension.
Narrative Tension & Forward Pull (new dimension: every line earns continued reading): same fragment treatment — period instead of comma sustains the staccato tension rhythm.
Narrative Tension & Forward Pull (description updated): capitalization consistent with new fragment structure.

reviewed_changes.original_text: the cycle of fixing one thing and irritating another. Most people notice it the same way —
reviewed_changes.new_text: the cycle of fixing one thing and irritating another. We started there, and built formulas around one goal: less reactivity — so your skin can stop defending itself and start behaving like it belongs to someone who isn't at war with their own face. Most people notice it the same way —
reviewed_changes.reason: Benefits Over Features — Always (new dimension: every feature must be followed by its 'so what?'): the original 'less reactivity' was stated as a feature/goal without translating into what the reader actually feels or gains. Added the 'so what?' — 'so your skin can stop defending itself and start behaving like it belongs to someone who isn't at war with their own face' — which anchors the benefit in a felt, specific experience.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: 'So your skin can stop defending itself and start behaving like it belongs to someone who isn't at war with their own face' — that's trying too hard. It's the kind of line that sounds clever in a brief but reads as overwrought in actual copy. The 'so what' instinct is right, I always want the benefit spelled out, but this particular phrasing is too much. I'd want something that lands with more ease, less performance.

reviewed_changes.original_text: That's the thing about a real recommendation from someone who knows you: they wouldn't send you somewhere disappointing.
reviewed_changes.new_text: When something actually works, you stop thinking about your skin. That's the whole point.
reviewed_changes.reason: Category Distinctiveness (new criterion added, dimension: no clichés or stock phrases that appear routinely across the category): 'a real recommendation from someone who knows you: they wouldn't send you somewhere disappointing' reads as warm-but-generic — it could close any lifestyle brand's copy. Replaced with a closing that is specific to the product's core promise (reactive skin takes up mental bandwidth; real relief means it disappears from your mind), which is harder to swap onto a competitor's page.

reworded.name: Benefits Grounded in Substance
reworded.old: Copy spends the majority of its time on benefits, not features. But benefits must be grounded in something real — a plainly stated mechanism, a specific decision the brand made, or a concrete change the reader can picture. Evocative benefit language that never explains what actually changes reads as evasive. Every feature needs a 'so what?' translation, and every benefit needs at least a sentence of substance anchoring it.
reworded.new: Copy spends the majority of its time on benefits, not features. But benefits must be grounded in something real — a plainly stated mechanism, a specific decision the brand made, or a concrete change the reader can picture. Evocative benefit language that never explains what actually changes reads as evasive. Every feature needs a 'so what?' translation, and every benefit needs at least a sentence of substance anchoring it. A spec-sheet list of features with no benefit translation is a failure regardless of how accurate it is.

reworded.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
reworded.old: Every element — body copy, CTAs, subheads, social proof — feels like it belongs to the same argument and the same voice. No seams, no register shifts, no moments where it feels like 'the copy handed off to a different department.' CTAs grow organically from the preceding emotional beat so clicking feels like the natural exhale — and the momentum leading into the CTA never deflates or resolves before the reader is asked to act.
reworded.new: Every element — body copy, CTAs, subheads, social proof — feels like it belongs to the same argument and the same voice. No seams, no register shifts, no moments where it feels like 'the copy handed off to a different department.' CTAs grow organically from the preceding emotional beat so clicking feels like the natural exhale — and the momentum leading into the CTA never deflates or resolves before the reader is asked to act. There is exactly one primary CTA. If you can't decide what the one thing is, the strategy isn't clear yet.

dimensions_changed.name: Quiet Confidence in Tone
added_dims: Copy never talks down to the reader — no condescension, no over-explaining, no tone that implies the reader needs to be convinced they have a problem

dimensions_changed.name: Benefits Grounded in Substance
added_dims: No feature is listed without a benefit translation — the copy never reads like a spec sheet, even briefly

dimensions_changed.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
added_dims: NOT clicking the CTA feels like leaving something unfinished — the copy above it creates that pull, not just the button itself

dimensions_changed.name: Category Distinctiveness
added_dims: Claims are specific enough that they could not apply to every product in the category — vague superlatives are a red flag

reasoning: I made three main moves: I bumped 'Quiet Confidence in Tone' up to priority 2 because avoiding hype, not talking down to people, and that smart-friend voice are really the foundation everything else rests on — it felt wrong to have it sitting at 6. I added a 'never talks down to the reader' dimension there because that's a genuine dealbreaker for me that wasn't explicitly called out. I also added a dimension to 'Seamless Persuasive Thread' about the CTA pull feeling like it comes from the copy above it, not just the button — that's the thing I actually check for. Small wording tweak to 'Benefits Grounded in Substance' to make the spec-sheet failure mode more explicit, and added a redundancy-catching dimension there too since I see that mistake constantly.
